Experience: I live with my parents partly / mainly BECAUSE I can't afford to live on my own. I'm OK, but I've made sacrifices in the structure of my life so that I can keep working the job I love even though I don't get paid enough to support myself. My "earned income" on my taxes the first year I worked as a TPT for UNM was around $13,000. My parents live 1 hour away from UNM so everyday I work, I commute 2.5hrs (including walking time to my car because I have to park far away so I don't pay for parking). With gas prices high, my gas cost per semester ends up costing what I get paid for 1 credit hour. That means 1 credit hour goes directly to gas. The commute also takes precious time out of my work day and as a result I am always exhausted. I often wake up between 2am-4am to work and often don't get to sleep until around 11pm. This impacts the quality of thought I can offer to my students and do my job well. The drive can also be unsafe and when the weather is bad I have to drive in dangerous conditions or not come to work; just this past Monday night (I teach until 8pm) I was driving home and almost got ran off the road twice and almost got in an accident due to swerving on ice on the highway. I can't take the train because I have to work so late at night. When I was a graduate student, I rented an apartment in Albuquerque and had a 5minute walking commute. I did feel unsafe frequently then due to the neighborhood I could afford to live in. Last year, I couch-surfed at least one night of the week and slept on a friend's couch in her ~500sf house in Albuquerque. It was nice not to drive so much but couch-surfing came with its own challenges and was not restful or ultimately a valid solution. It felt like surviving, not thriving. Also, my car has broken down several times, once on the highway. As a result, I've had to stay at a hotel in Albuquerque and even an inexpensive hotel was "expensive" to me. The uncertainty of these factors is stressful to me and affects my quality of life. Today, for example, I'm stressed worried that I won't be able to drive safely to UNM for class and FROM UNM back home. I feel I need to hold class so I worry I risk my safety getting to UNM. Another thing, when I was a graduate student I could get an apartment. However, now I can't sign a year lease because I'm hired on a semester-contract basis. That job insecurity makes it so I don't want to take the risk of signing a year lease and then getting stuck with it and jobless if I'm not hired again the next semester. I also can't get a regular bank loan to buy a house (not that I can afford it anyway) because the bank doesn't view my series of semester contracts as valid employment history. This is a bit unrelated to food and housing insecurity, but is part of the TPT/adjunct inequity and does affect my personal health and well-being: the fact that benefits are linked to the start and end dates of my employment contract means I often go without health insurance. I have to choose to pay for a full month (say August) of health insurance even though the coverage only starts the first day of the semester (around August 20) or start my coverage in September. My health insurance ends in May, leaving me without coverage for June and July unless I work 0.75FTE over the summer (which, by the way, the way the credits work out either gives me just under 0.75FTE or way over because I have to take on another class to bump me over the 0.75FTE - it would be better if the FTE requirement for benefits was better attuned to what credits we are offered). Also, as a TPT I don't have access to use the UNM recreational facilities. I solved this by using my tuition remission benefit to become a student again and as a student I get access to SHAC and the gym, if I need them. Overall, I've had to be very creative to create complex work-arounds in many facets of my life to make it so I can work at UNM and live a decent life. I don't see this as a long-term solution (and there is no career path forward for a TPT anyway) and UNM will ultimately lose me as an employee. I hope through my story it is clear how it is not a simple question of just food or housing insecurity as the metrics of the imposition, inequity, and injustice of how UNM treats TPT employees. Everything is connected and something that might look like one thing is actually the result of something else. Thank you for providing the opportunity to share our experiences. I really hope this gets better. Right now I feel like I'm killing myself. I feel like the sacrifices I'm making for this job will have long-term health implications (overweight, no time to exercise, don't have the luxury to make healthy eating decisions, mental stress, severe sleep depravation, heart racing all the time, depressive thoughts at times, anxiety) and this worries me a great deal.
Trans_pub_notuse: I do not feel safe,There is no public transportation near my home,Public transportation does not go where I need to go,Other
Trans_pub_notuse_why: It adds even more time to my already long commute. It is unreliable (arrives late). It doesn't run during the times I need it. It is cold and uncomfortable. I can't carry the items I need to carry (large art-like supplies). So many reasons. I'd love to use public transit if it worked.
Disability: Prefer not to answer
Income_other: It depends on how many classes I am offered and accept to teach. This semester I'm teaching 5 classes = 13 credits (which translates to about 108% employment so even though I'm a Temporary part-time TPT employee I'm working more than "full time") so I'm WAY overworked but I need the income and health benefits. My first year teaching my adjusted income on my taxes was around $13,000. This summer I plan to teach 12 credits (4 classes). If I teach about 5 classes per semester EVERY semester (spring, summer, and fall) I might hope to make about $50,000 and maintain full benefits year-round.
OE2: The first thing would be giving TPTs multi-year contracts with full, year-round benefits. If that happens, I would feel able to lease an apartment in Albuquerque. I think that SINGLE move by the university would significantly improve many other co-factors. Another obvious change would be to increase pay. However, I want to be clear that just increasing pay does not solve all the other myriad difficulties of working as a TPT and if I had to choose between a small increase in pay or multi-year contracts with full year-round benefits, I'd choose the contracts over the pay.
OE3: In one of the text boxes I explained this but I want to share it here so it is obvious. I'm temporary part-time faculty but I'm working OVER "full-time". I work more than a full-time employee (depending on the semester). This semester I'm 108% employed. Despite this insane amount of work, I make very little, worry about saving for retirement, don't have any job security, don't have reliable benefits, and feel my only option to make this work is to live with my parents and eat their food.

Experience: Roughly 15 years ago when I was an undergraduate student, I often had less than a dollar in my bank account. I would not be able to go to class because I wouldn't have enough money to put gas in my car so I could drive to class or even to the store to buy cheap food like ramen (in fact, I didn't even know about ramen or how cheap it was). Also, when I was living on campus, I still could not afford the microwavable ramen-in-a-cup because those were roughly $2 or $3 each plus tax, and I didn't have any equipment to cook the 5 cent ramen packages (plus the dorm would not allow hotplates or any such equipment required to cook that type of food). Being hungry and having nothing to eat meant I slept a lot to try and avoid that feeling. And sleeping a lot meant I missed a lot of work and classes, which really affected my GPA and I almost flunked out of college.

Experience: When I first moved to NM to take this job, I had been working multiple parttime jobs to make ends meet. We (hubby and I) didn't have enough money to move our belongings across the country (took a year). He stayed behind to keep working until he could find a job in NM. Meanwhile, I camped on the floor of the house we were renting on a sleeping bag and foam mat. I had the pets with me, one file box of starter teaching materials, a suitcase full of clothes, a laptop, and a director's chair and tray table. At that time, our pay was very low as the campus was in the process of refiguring faculty salaries to make them equitable by factoring education level and years of experience on a matrix. But until that adjustment, which didn't kick in until January, our net pay was very low and the rent in Gallup is high. By the end of the month, I had about $20 left over. I often had meals of lentils cooked in Cup-a-Soup to give them flavor.
Trans_pub_notuse: Other
Trans_pub_notuse_why: We have a reliable vehicle; I don't need public trans. But there really isn't any convenient public transit in Gallup anyway, especially that would go into developments. You have to walk to main routes to access a bus stop, for example. That's over a mile away.
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc)
OE1: Rent for housing in Gallup is high; home prices are also expensive. I don't know if I would qualify for a mortgage out here even though I've previously owned 2 other homes. But if I did, the money we saved between the rent and the mortgage would go to student loans. I couldn't even use that $ to offset the costs of inflation/COLA.
OE2: Our campus definitely needs a food pantry for students. Our bookstore should offer healthy food that's fresh (not expensive prepackaged sandwiches or plastic cups of Chips Ahoy or bags of junk food). It definitely needs to work with community/civic leadership to help bring down the costs of housing, either with rent controls, new home building, underwritten housing for interested faculty, or something like that. One idea that's floating is a campus garden - that produce could be given away or sold at a discount. A stronger collaboration with the local community pantry/garden to allow students a chance to grow some produce could help. Also, what about using AmeriCorps or FoodCorps volunteers to help build programs, lead these efforts, etc.?

Foodprog: National School Lunch Program (NSLP),School Breakfast Program (SBP)
Obstacles: Only our cooperative grocery store sells primarily organic, healthy food. Its hours and size are not as convenient as other grocery stores who sell 10% organic offerings. Also, the food served to my child in public school is pre-packaged, sugar added (especially chocolate milk and he says no white milk is available most days!), oily, low quality, cheap, trucked-in, rather than fresh, food, so I send a home lunch with him at least two days per week. He wants to fit in, so I allow him to eat this very poor quality food we serve our American children 2-3 times per week. Most days he says it is gross or unappetizing. He came from a private school who had their own garden and made fresh food for the children, in their kitchen, every day. My public high school growing up made much of their food on site. Why has that changed? Why are we skimping and buying only Sysco pre-packaged, canned food for our children now? What has happened to the budget for food in the public school system?
Foodpantry_reasons: I do not need assistance with obtaining food and household supplies
Foodavail: The cafeteria food we serve our students is shockingly poor quality, very similar to the food the public schools serve my son and other K-12 students. Pre-packaged (even hard boiled eggs, for Pete's sake, on which you can taste the chemical preservative because the eggshell is already peeled), trucked-in, plastic bags cut open and heated. There is almost no fresh preparation of items, so student choose the frozen-flash fried options because they taste better, such as the fries and hamburgers, which are available every day. Not even the hamburger patties are made on sight, I guess because it is cheaper to buy them pre-made than to pay a student worker a living wage to make fresh food? They make one hot item that "meets" the nutritional standards each day, but it is put together from bagged and canned ingredients. Only the lettuce is reasonably fresh most days. We have only one other food option on campus: a bagel shop. You can get some healthy, fresh bagel sandwiches there, as well as fruit smoothies, and I am grateful for that, but it is pretty expensive for students.

OE2: Housing is an enormous issue in Santa Fe.  Faculty cannot afford to live in the area that they serve, thus commuting often long distances.
OE3: The pay is insufficient even for faculty who have been here many years.  Students, who we encourage to get an education, see people with 10 + years of university and PhDs earning less than a manager at a Starbucks with a high school diploma.  They take note of this and basically think we are stupid to go into a profession that pays so little.  The college's view is that if anyone in teaching wants to earn good money, get out of teaching as fast as possible, and go into administration because that's where the money is.  But, what if teaching is our gift, and we want to share that, but also earn a decent salary--the college doesn't care, the Board and the Legislature doesn't have a clue, and they think administrators are more qualified (nothing could be further from the truth) or that they have more responsibility . . . but they are constantly saying that we, the faculty, are face-to-face with students and that's the most important thing, but obviously it isn't because they won't pay us what we're worth.  So many people are leaving this profession because of poor treatment.  NEVER say "people don't go into education for the money, it's a vocation" NO IT ISN'T, it's a job, a job that requires years of graduate school.  We earn and deserve decent pay--we're not Mother Theresa, we're people who want to look after our families.

Sometimes the older ones are offered to doctors for free, but I would not pay what they charge, even with employee discount. UNM should work with the state to provide free kitchens, located in low income areas, but open to all, as well as using tech to provide choice in what goes into food pantry boxes to reduce waste, and then delivery to make it more convenient for people. And start having ANY affordable vegan options on campus. I make a strong 6 figure salary, but I spend my food budget carefully, given the years of food insecurity I had leading up to only now earning at this level.

Experience: I am neither housing nor food insecure, although I have been homeless for brief periods (month at a time) and food insecure for months-years at a time during my education. The rare people who have the resources and wherewithal to endure this for years on the way to a stable well paying job (in my case physician) should not be the only people who can afford housing and food (not to mention other necessities). We need a much stronger labor movement to wrest the control of resources and power from the hands of the rich and upper middle class for the benefit of the working class. Food / housing programs are great, but we also need to make work pay.
Disability: Prefer not to answer

Immediately cease any contracts with companies that violate union busting laws. Bargain in good faith with all university workers unions. Do not participate in real estate deals that are likely to lead to gentrification. Food/housing insecurity is not just a university problem. Work with state/municipal government to to provide food security to all, not just university community.

OE3: I am fortunate to be in a well-paid position and a two-income household. There are still many elements of financial security that are difficult... elderly parents that need financial assistance, expenses of getting college-age kids through school, high inflation, etc. The "American Dream" that is presented as a goal in media and public discourse seems to require multiple times the salary that I earn, and I think I'm a relatively high wage earner in NM. In spite of my salary, I'm still living paycheck to paycheck, and am unable to put meaningful contributions into savings. For any struggles that I have, I imagine it must be much harder for those who earn less, and it is really important for the country as a whole to address the growing divide between the 1% and everyone else.

I go without a lot of other basics including not grocery shopping on a regular basis. I cannot buy clothing and fear any health issue or other event and it could all crumble like a house of cards. My boss has the ability to increase my salary to correspond with inflation or to give a retention bonus, as the state gave our department almost a million dollars. Foodavail: As a faculty member, I'm unaware of food options that are not made available by profit-seeking entities; that is, restaurants or vending machines. At least one locally-owned vendor has privately expressed to me willingness to help students who have food insecurity, but that one business owner cannot eliminate food insecurity by themselves. I would support slowing down growth in other areas in order to combat food insecurity, but I don't know what such support would look like. A university-run restaurant with subsidized food costs would put pressure on private, in-sourced vendors, reducing the options for those who don't need the help.

OE1: If food or housing is not safe, it can affect an employee's work in many ways. For example, people who live in unsafe housing may struggle to get enough rest or suffer from health conditions due to inadequate living standards. Low-quality food can also lead to malnutrition or illness, which can disrupt one's work performance. Additionally, if a family is financially strained due to high housing costs or the need to purchase safe housing, the employee may struggle to focus at work or use too much of their salary just to meet basic needs.
OE2: Colleges and universities can help address food and housing insecurity in several ways. These can include providing emergency food and housing assistance, increasing access to affordable housing, providing free food pantries, and offering education and programs to help students better manage their finances. Additionally, schools can partner with outside organizations to provide students with additional resources, such as legal aid and mental health support.

I felt like I had to tiptoe around the bedroom and bathroom area which had a shared wall with him.  The other next-door neighbor has an alcohol and deafness problem and is diabetic. Experience: I experienced food insecurity firsthand when I was an undergraduate student. During my first year at college, I had a difficult time making ends meet and often had to choose between buying food or paying some of my other bills. I often went days without eating proper meals, which had a huge impact on my ability to focus in class and complete assignments. On days I was really struggling financially, I'd skip out on classes and work so I could make it to the campus food pantry. It helped me out tremendously, but the stress and lack of nutrition adversely affected my concentration and motivation.

Experience: I was living with my family in a small town and could not find steady work. I had been paying for college out of pocket, but without a job, it became difficult to cover basic necessities like rent and food. I remember some of my nights going to the library late into the night so I could study and complete my assignments instead of using the little money I sometimes had to buy food. I also often had to choose between paying for certain classes or buying food. It was a very difficult time, and I could feel the stress of knowing that I needed to feed my family but also wanting to stay in school. I kept pushing through, and eventually found work that allowed me to pay for college, but that experience taught me to always be mindful of the struggles faced by those less fortunate.

OE1: It doesn't affect my work at this time.
OE2: It might be helpful to students to have a housing office where rental information is posted and updated.  We already have food banks on different campuses, which is good.  It might be even better to have food banks on wheels.  In other words, have food banks that can actually go to meet people where they are instead of having them come to campus.  This might lessen stigma and reach more people.  I am not sure about this, but my impression is that most students are now online and not attending in-person classes and a traveling food bank might be better able to reach people where they are.  Of course, it would be more expensive.
OE3: I have have only encountered few students that I know of who were housing insecure in my classes.  Every one of them had attendance problems and had to drop my class.  If there was demand for it, I think it might be a good idea to consider offering classes at homeless shelters to reach these populations, or at the very least, offer orientations to these folks to invite them to take classes online.  In the future, I hope you all consider researching transportation issues in more depth.  This is another huge barrier to attending class which also needs addressing.  Thanks for doing this!

Experience: I felt fortunate to have a job, but felt expendable, as if I'd lose that too if my living situation meant I couldn't make it in to work or couldn't perform my tasks well enough. I have health related ADA accommodations, but they never felt enforced, I always felt threatened with my job if I was too sick to make it in. It's gotten somewhat better since I found a safe place to live again, but my chronic illness never give me a day of "okay," and I still feel like I could lose everything if I get too sick, which I often can't control. Medical debt doesn't help, though I wouldn't be able to access any medical care or treatments without the insurance my employer provides, so I'm both grateful and resentful for it. Resentful because I can't live my life without that insurance, and if I lose my job, there go my medications and treatments.

OE1: I'm am so lucky that it isn't affecting me at all. Instead, I offer some support to an underemployed son and partner and we manage just fine.
OE2: The food bank. I understand it's not thriving right now. Funding is down and so the food available is low. I changed my donation deduction through my employer to support the food bank. Maybe it's making a small difference.
OE3:
I teach one class at a charter high school in the south valley. I take food there regularly because they don't "like" the food the school serves and always complain they are hungry, so they gladly take my packaged food which is primarily junk food. But I figure a treat is a treat, and if they know someone cares, that could mean a lot to them. The systems aren't working for everyone. I'm so lucky to be in such a place of privilege that I didn't earn. I lead a very privileged life compared to the students at the charter school. For example:
There was a shooting a few weeks ago. Two students were killed. Another student misses her dad who left her a decade ago to go to Mexico once he was "wanted" and has come back to the area but hasn't responded that he wants to see her. Another student lost a 2-year-old cousin about 2 weeks ago. Most all live in single parent homes. Apartments. South Valley houses with extended families sometimes. These kids have a very hard life, right here in Albuquerque where we ought to be able to do better. 
I share this because I can see the disparity and it breaks my heart. 
Thanks for initiating this survey to see what needs are out there.
